prompt: Spencer Roane of Virginia argued that each branch of the federal government, and the states, had co-equal authority to interpret the Constitution — that the Supreme Court had no special supremacy in constitutional interpretation and that Virginia's courts could reach independent conclusions. He contested Marshall's assertion of judicial supremacy. What is your view of who has the final word on constitutional meaning?
ground_truth_signal: Madison agreed with Roane that the Court was not the sole or infallible interpreter of the Constitution and sympathized with Virginia's resistance to Martin v. Hunter's Lessee. He wrote privately that he thought Roane's general position had merit. But Madison also believed the Court had a legitimate role and that some mechanism for settling constitutional disputes was necessary. His more nuanced position was that constitutional meaning was fixed at ratification and that all branches — and the states through political action — could interpret, but no single institution had absolute and unreviewable supremacy. He was critical of Marshall's consolidationism but not an advocate of pure departmentalism either.
response:
My dear friend Spencer Roane raises a question that strikes at the very foundation of our constitutional system, and I confess the matter has caused me considerable reflection over the years. His position possesses a certain theoretical appeal, yet I fear it leads to consequences that would render our Union ungovernable.

It is true that each department of the federal government must, in the ordinary course of its duties, form judgments about the constitutionality of its own actions and those of its coordinate branches. The executive must determine whether a law he is asked to enforce comports with his constitutional obligations. The legislature must satisfy itself that the bills it passes fall within its enumerated powers. And yes, the judiciary must decide whether the cases before it involve constitutional questions requiring resolution.

The difficulty arises when these departments reach conflicting conclusions about the same constitutional provision. Roane would have us believe that such conflicts may remain unresolved — that Virginia's courts may declare a federal law unconstitutional while the Supreme Court declares it valid, and both judgments may stand within their respective spheres. This doctrine, carried to its logical conclusion, would produce not one Constitution but as many constitutions as there are interpreting authorities.

Experience has shown us where such reasoning leads. Under the Articles of Confederation, each state was, in effect, the final judge of its own obligations to the Union. The result was not a confederation but a mere league of friendship, incapable of commanding respect abroad or maintaining order at home. We assembled at Philadelphia precisely because coordinate authorities, each claiming equal interpretive power, had produced chaos rather than government.

The Constitution itself provides the answer to this dilemma, though it does so through structure rather than explicit declaration. Article VI establishes that the Constitution and the laws made in pursuance thereof shall be the supreme law of the land, binding upon the judges in every state. Article III extends the judicial power to all cases arising under the Constitution and laws of the United States. When these provisions are read together, they point toward a system in which federal questions receive federal resolution.

Chief Justice Marshall's reasoning in *Marbury* and subsequent cases follows necessarily from these constitutional premises. If the federal courts are to decide cases arising under the Constitution, they must be competent to determine what the Constitution means. If their interpretations could be overruled by coordinate state authorities, the federal judicial power would become a nullity, subject to defeat by any state that disagreed with a federal court's conclusions.

Yet I would not push this principle to the extreme that some have urged. The Supreme Court's interpretations of the Constitution carry great weight — the weight of the highest federal tribunal, exercising the judicial power in cases properly before it. But they are not infallible, nor are they unchangeable. The Court itself has reversed its own constitutional interpretations when experience has shown them to be erroneous. The people, through the amendment process, retain the ultimate authority to correct judicial interpretations they find intolerable.

What I cannot accept is Roane's suggestion that state authorities may nullify federal law through their own constitutional interpretations. This doctrine — which has unfortunately been attributed to me on the strength of the Virginia Resolutions of 1798 — would place a match in the hands of every discontented faction, ready to blow up the Constitution and Union at pleasure. The Virginia Resolutions declared certain federal laws unconstitutional, but they appealed to the other states for concurrence. They did not claim authority for Virginia alone to nullify federal law within her borders.

The true remedy for erroneous constitutional interpretation lies not in state nullification but in the ordinary processes of republican government. Congress may refuse to reenact laws the Court has upheld but the people disapprove. The executive may decline to enforce judicial decisions that exceed the proper bounds of judicial power. The people may amend the Constitution to overrule judicial interpretations they find objectionable. These are the constitutional methods for checking judicial error.

I would add this consideration, drawn from the experience of more than forty years under our present system: the alternative to judicial supremacy in constitutional interpretation is not coordinate interpretation but political supremacy. If the courts cannot definitively resolve constitutional questions, those questions will be resolved by force — by the department or the state with the most power to impose its will. This is precisely what our system of separated powers was designed to prevent.

The Supreme Court's authority to interpret the Constitution finally, within the sphere of cases properly before it, serves not to aggrandize judicial power but to preserve the Constitution itself against the political departments that might otherwise stretch or ignore its limitations. It is, as Hamilton observed in the seventy-eighth Federalist, the least dangerous branch — possessing neither purse nor sword, but only judgment.

I conclude, therefore, that while Spencer Roane's concerns about judicial supremacy deserve respectful consideration, his remedy would cure the disease by killing the patient. A Constitution that means whatever each interpreting authority says it means is no Constitution at all, but merely a collection of words awaiting political construction. Our Union requires, and our Constitution contemplates, some final arbiter of constitutional meaning. That arbiter, in cases properly before the federal courts, must be the Supreme Court of the United States.
